meaning_a: to influence (verb)
example_a: The weather affects my mood
example_b: The effect of the medicine was immediate
tip: Affect = Action (both start with A), Effect = End result (both start with E)

meaning_a: guidance or recommendations (noun)
example_a: She gave me good advice about my career
example_b: I advise you to study harder for the exam
tip: Advice is a noun (the noun), Advise is a verb (the verb) - remember the S

meaning_a: to misplace or be defeated (verb)
example_a: I don't want to lose my keys again
example_b: This button is loose on my shirt
tip: Lose has one O (gone), Loose has two O's (room to move)

meaning_a: at that time or next (adverb)
example_a: I lived in Paris, and then I moved to London
example_b: She is taller than her brother
tip: Then = Time, Than = compArison

meaning_a: possessive pronoun showing ownership
example_a: Is this your book?
example_b: You're doing a great job
tip: You're = You Are, Your = belongs to you

meaning_a: possessive pronoun showing ownership of something
example_a: The dog lost its toy
example_b: It's a beautiful day
tip: It's = It Is, Its = belongs to it

meaning_a: possessive pronoun (belonging to them)
example_a: Their house is beautiful
example_b: The books are over there
tip: Their = ownership, There = location (here/there), They're = they are

meaning_a: to receive or agree to something (verb)
example_a: I accept your apology
example_b: Everyone came except John
tip: Accept = receive it, Except = exclude it

meaning_a: something that completes or pairs well with something else
example_a: The wine complements the meal perfectly
example_b: She gave me a compliment about my outfit
tip: Complement = COMPLETE, Compliment = i like it

meaning_a: a person in charge (noun) or main/most important (adjective)
example_a: The principal of the school gave a speech
example_b: I live by the principle of honesty
tip: Principal = PAL (the school principal is your pal), Principle = a rule

meaning_a: not moving or not movable
meaning_b: writing materials like paper and pens
example_a: The car remained stationary during the parking test
example_b: I bought some stationery for my office
tip: Stationary = not moving (ARy), Stationery = stEREotype of office supplies (E=Er)

meaning_a: atmospheric conditions (rain, sun, snow, etc.)
meaning_b: used to express doubt or choice between alternatives
example_a: The weather is sunny today
example_b: I don't know whether to go or stay
tip: Weather = rain/sun (wEAr it like a jacket), Whether = we have a choice

meaning_a: contraction of 'who is' or 'who has'
example_a: Who's going to the party?
example_b: Whose book is this?
tip: Who's = Who Is, Whose = belonging to whom

meaning_a: to place or put something down (transitive verb, needs an object)
meaning_b: to recline or be in a horizontal position (intransitive verb)
example_a: Lay your books on the table
example_b: I'm going to lie down and rest
tip: Lay needs an object, Lie does not. Lay the paper DOWN, Lie DOWN yourself

meaning_a: used for countable items (plural nouns)
example_a: There are fewer apples than oranges
example_b: I have less time than yesterday
tip: Fewer = count things (items you can number), Less = amount you can't count

meaning_a: greater physical distance
example_a: My house is farther from the city than yours
example_b: Further discussion is needed on this topic
tip: Farther = physical distance (FAR), Further = more/additional (goes further in idea)

meaning_a: surrounded by more than two things or people
meaning_b: in the space or interval separating two things
example_a: I felt alone among the crowd
example_b: The secret is between you and me
tip: Among = group of 3+ (think: 'a mong' = multiple), Between = two items

meaning_a: to carry something toward the speaker
meaning_b: to carry something away from the speaker
example_a: Please bring the documents to my office
example_b: Take your umbrella with you when you leave
tip: Bring = toward you (come in), Take = away from you (go out)

meaning_a: to take something with the intention of returning it
meaning_b: to give something with the expectation it will be returned
example_a: Can I borrow your pen?
example_b: I will lend you my book if you promise to return it
tip: Borrow = you take from someone, Lend = you give to someone

meaning_a: to move something upward (transitive verb, needs an object)
meaning_b: to move upward on one's own (intransitive verb)
example_a: Raise your hand if you know the answer
example_b: The sun will rise at 6 AM tomorrow
tip: Raise = you lift it up, Rise = it goes up by itself

meaning_a: to be in a seated position (intransitive verb)
meaning_b: to place something somewhere (transitive verb, needs an object)
example_a: Please sit down while we talk
example_b: Set the table for dinner
tip: Sit = you go down (I sit), Set = you put things down (you set things)

meaning_a: important in history, memorable
meaning_b: relating to history or the past
example_a: The moon landing was a historic moment
example_b: I am interested in historical facts about ancient Rome
tip: Historic = important/memorable in history, Historical = about history in general

meaning_a: to leave one's own country to settle in another
meaning_b: to enter another country to settle there
example_a: My grandfather emigrated from Italy
example_b: Many people immigrate to the United States seeking opportunities
tip: Emigrate = exit (EM = exit my country), Immigrate = in (IM = into new country)

meaning_a: to tell someone confidently that something is true
meaning_b: to make certain that something happens
example_a: I assure you that everything will be fine
example_b: Ensure you lock the door before leaving
tip: Assure = promise to a person, Ensure = make it happen, Insure = get insurance

meaning_a: at the side of, next to someone or something
meaning_b: in addition to, moreover, apart from
example_a: She sat beside me in the movie
example_b: Besides, I don't think that's a good idea
tip: Beside = next to location, Besides = also/too/additionally

meaning_a: impartial, not having a personal interest in the outcome
example_a: We need a disinterested judge to make this decision
example_b: The students seemed uninterested in the subject
tip: Disinterested = fair/impartial, Uninterested = bored/indifferent

meaning_a: ordinary, common, usual (adjective)
example_a: These are everyday items you can find in any store
example_b: I exercise every day
tip: Everyday = common/ordinary, Every day = each single day

meaning_a: adjective describing quality or state
meaning_b: adverb describing how something is done
example_a: That's a good movie
example_b: She plays the piano well
tip: Good = adjective (describes nouns), Well = adverb (describes verbs)

meaning_a: executed by rope (past tense of hang, specific meaning)
meaning_b: suspended or attached (past tense of hang, general meaning)
example_a: The criminal was hanged for his crime
example_b: She hung her coat on the hook
tip: Hanged = execution, Hung = all other meanings of hang

meaning_a: to suggest or express indirectly
example_a: He implied that he wasn't happy with the decision
example_b: From her tone, I inferred that she was angry
tip: Imply = I say indirectly (you imply), Infer = I figure it out (you infer from hints)

meaning_a: actually, in a factual way, word for word
meaning_b: using figures of speech, not meant literally
example_a: That building is literally on fire right now
example_b: I'm figuratively dying of laughter
tip: Literally = it's actually true, Figuratively = it's a comparison/exaggeration

meaning_a: to come before something
example_a: The appetizer precedes the main course
example_b: You may proceed with the experiment
tip: Precede = come beFORE, Proceed = move onWARD (proGRESS onward)

meaning_a: used to introduce the second part of a comparison
meaning_b: at a time in the past or future
example_a: I'm older than you are
example_b: Back then, nobody had internet
tip: Than = compArison (has an A), Then = Time (happens next)

meaning_a: used as the subject of a sentence or clause
meaning_b: used as the object of a verb or preposition
example_a: Who is going to the party?
example_b: To whom should I give this letter?
tip: Who = subject (like HE), Whom = object (like HIM) - remember the M

meaning_a: to refer to something indirectly
meaning_b: to evade or escape from something
example_a: In her speech, she alluded to recent political events
example_b: The criminal managed to elude the police
tip: Allude = mention indirectly (ALL-ude = hints), Elude = escape (E-LUDE = escape)

meaning_a: without stopping, uninterrupted
example_a: The pump provides a continuous flow of water
example_b: We had continual interruptions during the meeting
tip: Continuous = never stops, Continual = keeps happening again and again

meaning_a: a dry, sandy region with little rain (noun) or to abandon (verb)
meaning_b: a sweet course eaten after a main meal
example_a: The Sahara is a vast desert
example_b: I ordered chocolate cake for dessert
tip: Desert = sandy place (one S), Dessert = sweet stuff (extra S for sugary)

meaning_a: to draw out or bring out information or a response
example_a: The teacher elicited responses from the students
example_b: Drug smuggling is an illicit activity
tip: Elicit = draw it OUT (E-LICIT), Illicit = ILLegal (I-LLICIT)

meaning_a: relating to an individual or private matters
example_a: This is personal and I don't want to discuss it
example_b: The company hired new personnel for the project
tip: Personal = PERsonal to you, Personnel = people (with E at the end)

meaning_a: inner sense of right and wrong
example_a: My conscience wouldn't let me cheat on the exam
example_b: She remained conscious throughout the surgery
tip: Conscience = internal SCIENCE of morality (CoNsCIENCE), Conscious = aware
